coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive visual detection of mycotoxin citrinin with yellow-light emitting carbon dot and Congo red
coredata.dc:creator: Mandal, Saptarshi
Das, Prolay
coredata.dc:description: Ultrasensitive visual detection of mycotoxin citrinin is reported here using a novel yellow-light emitting Carbon dot (CD) and Congo red dye. Detection strategy is based on higher binding affinity of citrinin with diammonium citrate, and urea derived CD over Congo red in presence of Mg2+. Congo red statically quenched the yellow fluorescence of CD, resulting in emission in red region, which was restored in presence of citrinin. Visual limit of detection (LOD) of 10 pmol and spectroscopic LOD of 6.6 pmol/mL was achieved within 5 min of incubation with citrinin. The assembly was found to respond well for citrinin detection in the presence of Aflatoxin b1 (AFL b1) and Ochratoxin A (OTA), that frequently co-exist with citrinin in real food samples. The method exhibits immense potential to sense citrinin in sub-nanogram range by visual inspection in an inexpensive yet robust on-site detection platform in relevant food packaging and agricultural industry.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Citrinin
Mycotoxin
Congo red

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Calculation of fluorescence recovery and limit of detection (LOD)
Test with citrinin aptamer and spiked samples
Interaction of Congo red with CD
Interaction of citrinin with CD-Congo red assembly
Detection of citrinin in the presence of AFL b1, OTA and citrinin H1 and H2

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: P.D. thanks the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Patna for infrastructural and financial assistance. SM thanks IIT Patna for an Institute Research Fellowship. The authors acknowledge Central Surface Analytical Facility and SAIF IIT Bombay for instrumental facilities (XPS and TEM). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) pXRD, (B) FTIR spectra, and (C) and (D) TEM image of CD. E. XPS spectra and deconvoluted high-resolution XPS spectra of CD for C1s, N1s, and O1s.
A. UV–Vis absorption spectra of CD. B. Steady-state fluorescence spectra of CD. C. RP-HPLC chromatogram of CD.

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CD (30 µg/mL)-Congo red mixture with gradual increase in Congo red concentration (0–30 nmol/mL) (B) steady-state fluorescence CD-Congo red mixture with similar concentration variation (C) fluorescence lifetime spectra of CD (30 µg/mL) -Congo red (20 nmol/mL) mixture. D. Visual change of CD fluorescence after addition of Congo red (irradiated with a UV lamp). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CD-Congo red-Mg2+ in presence of increasing concentration of citrinin (0–75 pmol/mL) (B) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of CD-Mg2+-Congo red-Citrinin with similar concentration variation of (C) fluorescence enhancement of CD-Congo red solution as a function of citrinin concentration (pmol/ml), inset: calculation of LOD (
F A
F
CR
− 1) from fluorescence data. (D) Steady-state fluorescence spectra of CD and CD-Mg2+-Congo red in the presence of pre-incubated citrinin (0–75 pmol/mL) with citrinin-aptamer. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
. Scheme for Synthesis and purification of yellow light-emitting CD.

. A. Plausible mechanism of fluorescence recovery of CD in the presence of citrinin showing both electrostatic and pi-pi interaction between CD and Congo red. B. Detection strategy of citrinin in the presence and absence of AFL b1 and OTA.

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive visual detection of mycotoxin citrinin with yellow-light emitting carbon dot and Congo red

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Patna, Patna 801103, Bihar, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ultrasensitive visual detection of mycotoxin citrinin is reported here using a novel yellow-light emitting Carbon dot (CD) and Congo red dye. Detection strategy is based on higher binding affinity of citrinin with diammonium citrate, and urea derived CD over Congo red in presence of Mg2+. Congo red statically quenched the yellow fluorescence of CD, resulting in emission in red region, which was restored in presence of citrinin. Visual limit of detection (LOD) of 10 pmol and spectroscopic LOD of 6.6 pmol/mL was achieved within 5 min of incubation with citrinin. The assembly was found to respond well for citrinin detection in the presence of Aflatoxin b1 (AFL b1) and Ochratoxin A (OTA), that frequently co-exist with citrinin in real food samples. The method exhibits immense potential to sense citrinin in sub-nanogram range by visual inspection in an inexpensive yet robust on-site detection platform in relevant food packaging and agricultural industry.

ce:section.ce:para: Mycotoxins are low molecular weight structurally diverse secondary metabolites of filamentous fungi that exert carcinogenic, immunogenic, and other toxic effects on humans and animals in low concentration (Xu, Jia, Gu, & Sung, 2006). One of them is citrinin, a polyketide mycotoxin, first isolated from filamentous fungus Penicillium citrinum by Hetherington and Raistrick in 1931 (Hetherington & Raistrick, 1931). It is also produced by other species of Penicillium, Aspergillus, and Monascus (Xu et al., 2006). Natural occurrence of citrinin was reported in maize, barley, wheat, fruits (apples, grapes, pear), cheese, wine, nuts (almonds, peanuts, hazelnuts, and pistachios), spices (turmeric, coriander, black pepper, cardamom, cumin) (Ostry, Malir, & Ruprich, 2013). Though citrinin has antimicrobial properties, its nephrotoxic, hepatotoxic and genotoxic effects make it a dreadful mycotoxin (Xu et al., 2006; Doughari, 2015. Herein, we report a rapid, sensitive and robust platform to visually detect the presence of citrinin with novel yellow emitting Carbon Quantum Dot (CQD) or simply Carbon Dot (CD) in alliance with Congo red dye.

So far, commonly used techniques to analyze citrinin are thin-layer chromatography (TLC) (Gimeno, 1984; Martins, Gimeno, Martins, & Bernardo, 2002), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Lepom, 1986; Phillips, Hayes, & Berndt, 1980; Vazquez et al., 1996), enzyme immunoassays (EIA) (Abramson, Usleber, & Märtlbauer, 1995), Raman Spectroscopy (Singh et al., 2014) and mass spectroscopy (LC-MS, GC–MS) (Shu & Lin, 2002; Tuomi, Johnsson, Hintikka, & Reijula, 2001). Although these methods have high sensitivity, their high cost and complicated operations demand the implementation of new rapid, easy, and inexpensive detection techniques. Reportedly, simultaneous detection of Zearalenone and Citrinin by Voltammetry on Pyrolytic Graphite Electrode was achieved in µM range of mycotoxin concentration (Nasir & Pumera, 2014). An ELISA based lateral-flow immunochromatographic assay strip for the detection of citrinin in cereals was reported that used gold nanoparticle labeled anti-citrinin antibodies as the probe (Kong, Xie, Liu, Song, & Kuang, 2017). Recently, Palladium nanoparticles functionalized graphene quantum dots with molecularly imprinted polymers have been developed to detect citrinin in nanomolar range on glassy carbon electrodes (Akyıldırım et al., 2017). Detection of citrinin has also been achieved on an aptasensing platform using pico green as a dye probe (Zhang et al., 2016). A micellar electrokinetic capillary chromatographic method was employed for simultaneous detection of citrinin and lovastatin in red yeast rice supplements (Nigović, Sertić, & Mornar, 2013). A surface-active maghemite nanoparticle was proposed as an efficient and reliable tool for recognition and removal of citrinin (Magro et al., 2016). Nanogram amount of citrinin was determined by a microsphere-based flow cytometric immunoassay (Li, Wu, Guo, Zheng, & Guo, 2012). Nevertheless, visually demonstrable yet ultrasensitive detection of citrinin in nanomolar range has remained an elusive target that could potentially lead to easy point-of-care solutions in food packaging, agriculture, and environmental fronts.

CD is the newest member in the pool of luminescence materials with various advantages like facile synthesis or fabrication, negligible photo-bleaching, stability, excellent biocompatibility, etc. (Shamsipur, Barati, & Karami, 2017). The photophysical properties of CD extant fascinating prospects for their uses in biomedical research as fluorescent probes, crosslinkers and delivery vehicles (Shamsipur et al., 2017; Singh et al., 2017). A FRET-based assay for detection of ochratoxin A (OTA) in agricultural products was reported that used nitrogen-doped CD as energy donor and DNA immobilized thiolated Ag-NPs as energy acceptor (Wang, Tan, & Chen, 2018). Elsewhere, graphene quantum dots were used for fluorescent-based aptasensing of OTA (Wang, Zhang, Pang, Zhang, & Guo, 2017). An assembly of CD and gold nanoparticles were also developed for sensitive detection of AFL b1 (Wang et al., 2016). These recent research initiatives suggest that CD could prove to be an important nanomaterial for developing novel sensory applications for detection of other mycotoxins as well (Nguyen, Tozlovanu, LuyenTran, & Pfohl-Leszkowiczb, 2007). However, CD is being frequently projected as excellent fluorescence probes without much exploration of surface chemistry. The wise choice of starting material for synthesis of CD may provide significant tools to preferentially trap/interact with particular types of molecules that could pave the way for selective binding and subsequent sensing with a measurable output.

For the first time, we report the synthesis of nitrogen-doped aqueous soluble CD from diammonium citrate and urea that emit in the yellow region. The nanoassembly reported here using this CD present a unique opportunity to confirm the presence of citrinin without using any antibody or aptamer, yet an aptamer was used for further confirmation subsequently. The yellow fluorescence of CD was quenched in the presence of Congo red dye, only to be recovered after addition of citrinin due to higher binding preferences of citrinin with CD over Congo red. Also, the detection strategy works well in presence of other mycotoxins like AFL b1, OTA that is mostly found along with citrinin in contaminated samples. The reported strategy is reproducible, sensitive and cost-effective, thus potentially be developed into an on-site visual detection sensing platform for citrinin.

Diammonium citrate and Congo red were purchased from Alfa Aesar. Rhodamine 6G, citrinin, OTA, AFL b1, and aptamer for citrinin were obtained from Merck. Urea, dimethylformamide, ethyl acetate and all other chemicals were procured from commercial sources and were used without further purification. Ultrapure water from Milli-Q was used as solvent in all experiments unless specified.

Solvothermal method was employed to prepare a yellow light-emitting CD. Briefly, 75 mg of diammonium citrate and 100 mg of urea (mole ratio 1:5) was dispersed in 3 ml dimethylformamide. The mixture was heated at 180 °C for 7 h in a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. After cooling to room temperature, the crude mixture was passed through a 0.22 µm syringe filter to remove large agglomerates. It was then further purified using water and ethyl acetate in a separating funnel. The water-soluble part contains yellow emitting CD, while the ethyl acetate part contains blue-emitting CD, as evident from UV illumination (302 nm). The water-soluble part was collected and stored at 4 °C for further use.

Powder XRD analysis was performed in Rigaku TTRX-III diffractometer with Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54 Å)as an X-ray source. TEM analysis was carried out on a Tecnai G2, F30 Transmission electron microscope. The diluted CD solution was applied on the carbon-coated copper TEM grid and dried at room temperature prior to measurement. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded in Perkin Elmer Spectrum 400. CD was pelletized with KBr in a hydraulic press and was scanned over a wavenumber range of 4000 cm−1 to 400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed in AXIS Supra (Kratos Analytical, UK) with monochromatic Al Kα as an X-ray source. For survey scans, pass energy was 160 eV with resolution about 2 eV, and for high-resolution scans, pass energy was 20 eV with resolution about 0.5 eV.

UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-2550 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Steady-state fluorescence measurements were performed at room temperature in the Fluoromax-4P spectrofluorometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon). Time-resolved fluorescence emission decays were measured at room temperature using picosecond time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) technique by a time-resolved fluorescence spectrophotometer (LifeSpec-II, Edinburgh Instruments, UK). Both absorption and fluorescence measurements were carried out in quartz cuvette with a 1 cm path length. Digital images were acquired with a Canon D-60 digital camera.

High-performance liquid chromatogram of CD solution was obtained in a reversed-phase high-performance liquid chromatography (RP-HPLC) system (controller: CBM-20Alite, pump: LC-20AD, PDA detector: SPD-M20A; Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) using an enable C18G column (250 × 4.6 mm). The solvent gradient was created using water, 10 mM Tris.HCl buffer (pH 7.4) and acetonitrile, while the flow rate was kept 0.4 ml/min. From start to 10 min, the water percentage was decreased from 85% to 55%, Tris–HCl buffer concentration was increased from 0 to 10%, and acetonitrile concentration was increased from 15% to 30%, then it was retained up to 30 min. The chromatogram was extracted at wavelength 270 nm. The injection volume added in the HPLC was 10 µL of CD solution (0.2 µg/µl).

The QY of the purified CD was determined by a comparative method using the following equation:
(1)
Φ x
=
Φ s
F x
f s
η
x
2
F s
f x
η
s
2

where subscript × and s denote test and standard sample respectively; Φ is fluorescence quantum yield; F is integrated fluorescence intensity; f is absorption factor (f = 1 – 10−A, where A = absorbance); and η is refractive index of solvent (1.33 for water and 1.36 for ethanol) (Brouwer, 2011). Rhodamine 6G (QY = 95% in ethanol) was used as a reference or standard. Absorbance in 10 mm fluorescence cuvette was kept below 0.1 at excitation wavelength to avoid internal filter effects and errors arising from uneven distribution of excited species in detected volume.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of fluorescence recovery and limit of detection (LOD)
ce:section.ce:para:
Fluorescence recovery of CD citrinin solution in the presence of Mg2+ after addition of citrinin was calculated using the equation:
(2)
F A
-
F
CR
F
CD
-
F
CR

Where FA is fluorescence of CD citrinin solution after the addition of analyte, FCR is fluorescence of CD-Congo red solution, and FCD is fluorescence of CD. For determination of LOD, a curve of fluorescence recovery versus concentration of analyte was plotted and fitted with a straight line. LOD of the analyte was calculated using the equation:
L O D =
3.3 × S D
b
, where SD is standard deviation of ordinate intercept, and b is slope of regression line (Shrivastava & Gupta, 2011).

ITC measurements were carried out at 25 °C using a MicroCal iTC200 from GE Healthcare. The concentration of CD was estimated by considering data obtained from TEM images and XPS data regarding the size of CD and C, O, and N content of the CD, respectively (Supplementary information). 200 µL CD solution (~200 µM) in 1 mM Mg2+ in water was loaded in the sample cell. 10 µM Congo red or 10 µM citrinin solution was taken in the syringe and titrated into the sample cell. ITC was carried out with 18 consecutive injections (2 μL) of Congo red or citrinin solution (10 μM) to mix with the CD (200 μL) with 2 min intervals between successive injections. The rotation speed of the syringe was maintained at 500 rpm. The integration of the heat pulses obtained from each titration was fitted using MicroCal analysis software coupled with Origin 7.0 to determine the site-binding model and get the corresponding binding constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Test with citrinin aptamer and spiked samples
ce:section.ce:para: Citrinin and related mycotoxins were pre-incubated with a citrinin specific aptamer 5′-GGCCAGGCGGGGCCTGTTCGCTGGGGCCGTGTCTTCGGCTCGCTCGGTGTTGTTTGGCG-3′ (Zhang et al., 2016). Any visual changes in fluorescence of CD-Congo red-Mg2+ solution after incubation with these pre-incubated mycotoxins were observed under UV irradiation of wavelength 302 nm. Fluorescence behavior was further investigated by performing steady-state fluorescence spectroscopic analysis.

Powdered rice and wheat samples (1 g) were spiked with mycotoxins (1 nmol) and then extracted with 10 ml of 70% methanol by ultrasonication for 15 min and centrifugation at 2000 rpm for 10 min following protocols mentioned by the European Food Safety Authority (EFSA Panel on Contaminants in the Food Chain (CONTAM), 2012). The supernatant was evaporated to dryness, and the residue was redissolved in acetonitrile. The extracted mycotoxin solutions were then used for the evaluation of our detection scheme.

We could not find any commercial sources for citrinin H1 and H2, so following literature we induced the formation of these degradation products by heating the citrinin solution in the presence of water at 100 °C for 20 min and then at 140 °C for another 5 min (Hirota, Menta, Yoneyama, & Kitabatake, 2002; Xu et al., 2006).

Synthesis of the yellow-light emitting CD was optimized by varying the molar mass ratio of diammonium citrate and urea, time, and temperature (Scheme 1 ). After synthesis of each sample, their fluorescence was recorded under UV light. Increasing molar mass ratio of diammonium citrate and urea from 1:3 to 1:5 resulted in increase in fluorescence intensity, which decreased eventually with further increase in the ratio to 1:7. The decrease in fluorescence intensity was also observed by increasing temperature from 180° to 210 °C and increasing time beyond 7 h. Thus, for our newly synthesized CD, optimum molar mass ratio, time, and temperature were fixed as 1:5, 7 h, and 180 °C, respectively. Both diammonium citrate and urea have poor solubility in DMF that lead to unwanted carbonization and results in the formation of blue light-emitting CD with compromised solubility in polar solvent. This prompted us to purify the desired yellow emitting CD by solvent extraction using two immiscible solvents, DMF water mixture, and ethyl acetate. The blue light-emitting CD appeared in the ethyl acetate layer and was discarded, while the yellow light-emitting CD remained in DMF water mixture and was collected for further studies.

Upon confirmation of yellow fluorescence emission from the aqueous layer in the separating funnel following CD synthesis using a UV lamp (wavelength 302 nm), the CD in the aqueous layer was further characterized by a host of analytical techniques. X-ray diffractogram in pXRD experiment of the CD showed two diffraction peaks around 28.7° and 39.2° corresponding to (0 0 2) and (1 0 0) planes of the graphitic framework (Fig. 1 A). Compared to the ordered crystal structure of graphite, the weak and broad diffraction peak with upward shift is attributed to highly disordered carbon and decrease in sp2 (CC) layer spacing that occurred in the carbonization process. Overall, the result indicates reduced crystallinity and heterogeneous multilayered structure of the CD (Du et al., 2014). Morphology and size of CD were investigated by TEM analysis (Fig. 1C and D). It showed nearly spherical nature of the CD along with average size of 5.2 nm. The high-resolution TEM image revealed turbostratic nature of our prepared CD. It disclosed clear lattice fringes embedded in the amorphous graphitic matrix. The lattice spacing of 0.33 nm and 0.24 nm was also observed correlating pXRD findings. Selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of CD unveiled amorphous and reduced crystalline nature of the CD consistent with pXRD revelations (Fig. S1, SI).

Surface functional groups of CD were determined by FTIR analysis that showed the presence of –NH2, –COOH and –OH groups (Fig. 1B). The peak at 3450 cm−1 was attributed to NH stretching of amine, and the broadness of the peak towards lower wavenumber denotes OH stretching vibration. Further, CO stretching peak at 1715 cm−1, OH bending peak at 1415 cm−1, and CO stretching peak at 1190 cm−1 denote the presence of alcoholic and carboxylic groups on the surface of the CD.

XPS characterization was performed to probe the surface states and elemental composition of CD (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2015). XPS survey spectrum of CD showed three typical peaks at 286 eV, 399 eV, and 531 eV corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 1E). The atomic concentration of C1s, N1s, and O1s was found to be 67.41%, 12.57%, and 20.02%. High-resolution spectra of C1s were deconvoluted into three peaks corresponding to sp2 carbons (CC, 284.2 eV), sp3 carbons (CO/CN, 286.3 eV) and carboxyl carbons (CO, 288.9 eV). N1s band was resolved into three peaks corresponding to pyridinic N (398.1 eV), amino N (399.5 eV), and pyrrolic N (400.5 eV). O1s band was deconvoluted into two peaks attributed to CO (531.7 eV) and CO (533.4 eV).

Electronic transitions in the CD were investigated by UV–Vis spectroscopy (Fig. 2 A). In the spectra, a shoulder at 272 nm corresponding to
π -
π
∗
transitions of CC and CN bonds of the sp2 C domain within the sp3 C matrix were observed. Absorption in the region of 300–500 nm with peaks at 343 nm and 420 nm are attributed to
n - π
∗
transitions of CO and CN bonds originating from trapping of excited-state energy by surface states on CD. The tailing in absorption band of CD towards higher wavelength is a consequence of several low energy transitions occurring on the surface of CD (Jones, Sahatiya, & Badhulika, 2017).

Steady-state fluorescence emission spectra of aqueous solution of CD showed slight excitation dependent fluorescence emission (Fig. 2B). The emission maximum was red-shifted from 518 nm to 566 nm as the excitation wavelength was changed from 365 nm to 500 nm. However, strongest fluorescence emission was observed at ~545 nm under excitation wavelength of 425 nm. The QY of CD in water was calculated as 38.7% with Rhodamine 6G as standard using Equation (1).

The purity of the prepared CD was evaluated by RP-HPLC analysis (Fig. 2C). The chromatogram showed two peaks at retention time 4.4 and 4.8 min with peak purity index of 0.99. This enumerated high purity and almost a homogenous nature of the prepared CD vis-à-vis a reliable methodology to check synthetic fidelity of CD was demonstrated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of Congo red with CD
ce:section.ce:para: Photo-physics of CD in the presence of Congo red were studied by UV–Vis absorption and steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy. UV–Vis absorption spectra of CD solution (30 µg/mL) after addition of different amounts of Congo red (0–30 nmol/mL) was measured that overshadowed the peak at 420 nm of CD with emergence of a new peak that gradually shifts towards red region settling at ~497 nm (Fig. 3 A). At low concentration, Congo Red molecules assemble on surface of CD by different non-covalent interactions, and then excess addition of Congo Red result in an increase in absorbance without any shift in wavelength. The steady-state fluorescence spectra of CD at different concentrations of Congo red show suppression in fluorescence intensity with the red shifting of emission maxima (Fig. 3B). As surface defects on CD were being overcrowded by Congo red molecules, red fluorescence of Congo red was observed with suppression of yellow fluorescence of CD.

To understand the static or dynamic nature of the interaction of Congo red with CD, time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopic measurements of CD (30 µg/mL), and CD-Congo red mixture (30 µg/mL of CD + 20 nmol/mL of Congo red) solution were performed. The obtained data were fitted with triple exponential function concerning instrument response factor (IRF) to get the life-time of the fluorophores present in respective solutions (Fig. 3C). CD only solution shows fluorescence life-time of 5.63 ns, whereas CD-Congo red solution shows fluorescence lifetime of 5.68 ns. It indicates that the quenching of CD fluorescence in the presence of Congo red is static in nature. The insignificant increase in fluorescence lifetime of CD-Congo red solution is due to additive effect of Congo red fluorescence lifetime. The suppression of yellow fluorescence of CD and gradual emergence of red fluorescence after addition of Congo red was also visually detectable in test tubes illuminated with UV lamp (Fig. 3D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction of citrinin with CD-Congo red assembly
ce:section.ce:para: When citrinin solution was incubated for at least 6 h with CD-Congo red solution, fluorescence emission changed from red to yellow as observed under UV irradiation. The visually detectable recovery of yellow fluorescence of CD after the addition of citrinin definitely hints towards a detection strategy of citrinin using the CD-Congo red assembly. However, due to the high incubation time (6 h or more), its practical application sounds tangential. To resolve this problem, coincubation with many cations like Na+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Co2+ and Mg2+ was tested. Mg2+ was found optimum for our experiments as it did not show any interference in control experiments. Most interestingly, the recovery of yellow fluorescence of CD in CD-Congo red Mg2+ mixture in the presence of citrinin was hastened and achieved in 5 min. Magnesium citrate is routinely used for extraction of mycotoxins for its superior chelating ability, which hints towards its plausible role in expediting the fluorescence recovery process (Jettanajit & Nhujak, 2016). Since Mg2+ has an affinity for a higher co-ordination number, citrinin molecules get attached on the surface of CDs through Mg2+ induced chelation that involves citrate/amine of the CD and β-Hydroxy/ β-keto acid functionality of citrinin from analyte displacing Congo red molecules from CD surface and initiating fluorescence recovery of CD (Scheme 2 A).

No change in steady-state fluorescence spectra of CD-Congo red was observed after the addition of Mg2+, and the visually detectable red fluorescence of CD-Congo red assembly remain unfazed. However, in UV–Vis absorption spectra of CD-Congo red-Mg2+ solution, hypsochromic shift corresponding to the
π -
π
∗
transition of azo group of Congo red from 497 nm to 483 nm was observed due to ionic effect (Fig. S2, SI) (Sladewski, Shafer, & Hoag, 2006). Overall absorbance of the CD-Congo red-Mg2+ solution decreased with the addition of citrinin solution up to ~ 50 pmol/mL beyond which the decrease was very low (Fig. 4 A). However, when concentration of Mg2+ is above 5 µmol/mL in CD-Congo red-Mg2+, a drastic change in spectral pattern of UV–Vis absorption of CD-Congo red-Mg2+-citrinin solution was observed at and beyond 50 pmol/mL of citrinin. Two new peaks at 525 nm and 580 nm instead of the peak at 483 nm were found to emerge (Fig. S3, SI). The decrease in absorbance intensity with red shift of Congo red monomer bands after addition of Mg2+ beyond 5 µmol/mL and citrinin is attributed to partial self-association of Congo red monomers that are released from the CD surface to minimize hydrophobic interaction with water (Nadjia, Abdelkader, & Ahmed, 2011). The Mg2+ concentration in the probe solution was thus optimized and maintained at 1 µmol/ml.

Fig. 4B shows steady-state fluorescence recovery of CD with successive addition of citrinin to the CD-Congo red-Mg2+ solution. The fluorescence emission maxima shift back from 570 nm to 545 nm after addition of citrinin. The fluorescence recovery of CD was negligible beyond 50 pmol/mL of citrinin incubation, which correlates well with the observations from UV–Vis absorption spectroscopic analysis. No change was observed in the fluorescence lifetime of CD-Congo red-Mg2+ after addition of citrinin (Fig. S4, SI).

To optimize fluorescence recovery after the addition of citrinin, concentration of Mg2+ and Congo red was titrated keeping CD concentration unchanged (Table S1, SI). An increase in the concentration of Congo red (20 nmol/mL) or Mg2+ (1 µmol/mL) reduced the efficiency of fluorescence recovery. All the experiments were performed at pH 6.8 ± 0.2 since increase or decrease in pH other than that creates interference and may provide false results. Decrease in pH resulted in native fluorescence enhancement of citrinin with decreased fluorescence intensity of CD, and additionally Congo red changed its emission from red to blue at pH below 3. An increase in pH towards basic condition resulted in increase in fluorescence intensity of CD and visual observation of red fluorescence in presence of Congo red was greatly interfered. A maximum of 56 ± 2% fluorescence recovery could be achieved with addition of citrinin to the solution of CD-Congo red-Mg2+ at neutral pH. For steady-state fluorometric detection of citrinin, LOD as low as 6.6 pmol/mL with linear range from 2.5 to 50 pmol/mL for citrinin was achieved using the optimized conditions (Fig. 4C, Equation (2)).

We introduced a citrinin specific aptamer to confirm the specificity of our detection strategy. The analyte solution was pre-incubated with citrinin aptamer that was followed by regular detection by the probe solution. No visual change in red fluorescence of CD-Congo red-Mg2+ solution was observed in the presence of the aforementioned pre-incubated solution if citrinin was present (Fig. 4D). Given the fact that the citrinin present in the analyte preferably binds with its aptamer, fluorescence recovery of CD was not possible in the presence of citrinin in the analyte. Steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy showed no significant change in fluorescence spectra of CD-Congo red-Mg2+-analyte solution. This served as a confirmatory test for the presence of citrinin only in the analyte.

To investigate the role of functional groups and core structure of citrinin for effecting fluorescence recovery of CD, we performed steady-state fluorescence spectroscopic measurements of CD-Congo red solution with l-serine, acetylacetone, and coumarin 6 (Fig. S5, SI). While l-Serine and acetylacetone have β-hydroxycarboxylic acid and β-keto carboxylic acid functionality respectively similar to citrinin, Coumarin 6 is a benzopyran that matches the core of citrinin structure. No significant change in fluorescence emission was observed with either l-Serine, Acetyl acetone, or Coumarin 6 even after incubation at higher concentration with CD-Congo red solution. Thus, evidently both the functional groups and core structure of citrinin are responsible for strong interaction with the ammonium citrate, and urea derived CD.

The thermodynamic parameter of binding of citrinin or Congo red with CD was further quantitatively evaluated by ITC experiments. ITC was performed using CD with Congo red and citrinin as titrant separately in the presence of 1 mM Mg2+. The thermogram obtained showed that binding of Congo red with CD-Mg2+ was endothermic, while binding of citrinin with CD-Mg2+ was exothermic in nature (Fig S6, SI). Due to the heterogeneous structure and possibility of the presence of a different number of binding sites on the CD surface, we found difficulty in obtaining excellent fitting curve. However, the best results for binding constants were obtained when raw data were fitted with three sequential binding site models. Binding constants for CD-Mg2+-citrinin was found to be 100 times higher compared to that for CD-Mg2+-Congo red (Table S2, SI). The findings from ITC data analysis prove beyond doubt that the binding preference of our newly synthesized CD with citrinin is much greater than Congo red that paves the way for efficient fluorescence recovery of CD after addition of citrinin by displacing Congo red from CD surface. We believe ITC studies with CD reported herein is first of its kind that helped us to quantitatively determine binding parameters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of citrinin in the presence of AFL b1, OTA and citrinin H1 and H2
ce:section.ce:para: Sometimes fungi that produce citrinin also yield other mycotoxins like AFL b1 and OTA. Hence, checking the sensitivity of our citrinin detection assembly in the presence of AFL b1 and OTA is obvious. Both AFL b1 and OTA have significant native blue fluorescence in our experimental pH 6.8. When AFL b1 and OTA were added to CD Congo red Mg2+ mixed solution and incubated for 5–10 min at room temperature, the fluorescence of the mixed solution changed from red to blue under UV irradiation (302 nm). Interactions of these two mycotoxins with CD and Congo red were investigated by steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. S7, SI). The nature of the curves for AFL b1 and OTA was found to have some resemblance to that of citrinin. There is a possibility of non-specific interactions of citrinin with CD-Congo red-Mg2+ in aqueous solution in the presence of closely associated mycotoxins like AFL b1 and OTA. When AFL b1 and OTA were present in analyte solution in addition to citrinin, fluorescence gradually changed from red to bluish yellow (distinct from the yellow fluorescence of CD in the pure and recovered form with only citrinin), but the recovery was inefficient. In this case, the change in fluorescence was due to the presence of AFL b1 and OTA. Our detection scheme was evaluated with citrinin spiked samples from rice and wheat where ~75% recovery was achieved. The samples showed similar results very much to that of analyte-containing pure citrinin and signified the viability of our detection scheme in food and feedstuff.

Citrinin H1 and H2 are degradation products of citrinin that are formed by boiling citrinin solution. Approximately 5 µL of degraded citrinin solution (10 pmol/µl) was required for visual detection of citrinin with our minimum recommended amount as opposed to only 1 µL untreated citrinin solution (10 pmol/µl) (Fig. S8, SI). Our detection strategy involved Mg2+ induced chelation with citrate/amine functionality of the CD and β-Hydroxy/ β-keto acid functionality of citrinin. Since citrinin H1 and H2 lack β-Hydroxy/ β-keto acid functionality, they were unable to displace Congo red molecules from CD surface. Thus, unlike AFL b1 and OTA, high or intermediate concentrations of citrinin H1 and H2 do not interfere with the detection of citrinin in our sensing strategy.

The overall detection strategy of citrinin is depicted in Scheme 2B, and the minimum recommended amount of components of the probe solution is presented in Table S3 (SI). Analyte-containing as much as 10 pmol/mL citrinin is sufficient for visual detection in a total sample volume of 10–12 µL without any machine intervention except a UV lamp. The contrast in the digital images point towards high robustness in visual detection of citrinin, and results obtained are highly reproducible. To obtain greater sensitivity, steady-state fluorescence spectra could be performed with the same solutions where citrinin as low as 6 pmol/mL could be detected.

Almost all mycotoxin biosensors use antibodies or aptamers as a detection element that essentially elevate the costing and operational complexity, as well as poses challenges to its shelf life, temperature sensitivity, and easy on-field readout of sensing outcome by non-technical person. These problems are more pronounced in developing nations having tropical climates where summer temperatures often reach beyond 40 °C. Herein, a biosensing platform to visually detect the presence of citrinin, a mycotoxin in picomole range is demonstrated with CD that does not require any antibodies or aptamer. A facile approach was presented for synthesis of the highly luminescent yellow emitting CD with moderately high quantum yield. The purity of the prepared CD was analyzed by a newfangled RP-HPLC profile. Static quenching was observed after addition of Congo red to the CD that suppressed the yellow fluorescence with the emergence of red fluorescence. The red fluorescence of CD-Congo red assembly was reverted to yellow in the presence of citrinin, which was greatly expedited in the presence of Mg2+. The visually detectable fluorescence recovery was attributed to the preferential binding of citrinin on the CD surface over Congo red, which was demonstrated by ITC experiments for the first time. The method outlined herein could detect citrinin in the presence of other related mycotoxins like AFL b1 and OTA in aqueous solutions and as well as methanolic extract of real foodstuff. Most importantly, the detection strategy is simple, inexpensive, robust and highly reproducible that could be achieved without any equipment or trained personnel. Picomole amount of citrinin had been detected by our biosensing platform with LOD of visual detection ~10 pmol/mL and 6.6 pmol/mL spectroscopically that only exploited the chemistry of interaction between the functional groups of citrinin and on the surface of the CD. Overall, we believe the detection strategy has the potential for successful translation into a preliminary yet highly sensitive on-site citrinin sensor in agro and food industry that could be further quantified using Mass Spectrometry strategies per requirement.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: P.D. thanks the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Patna for infrastructural and financial assistance. SM thanks IIT Patna for an Institute Research Fellowship. The authors acknowledge Central Surface Analytical Facility and SAIF IIT Bombay for instrumental facilities (XPS and TEM).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2019.126076.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
